fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the time-tracking plain-text engine we've been talking about. The core idea is that people write their work days in a simple text file — date at the top, some notes, then a list of time entries underneath — and the system can parse all of that, validate it, compute totals, and spit it back out in a clean format. Think of it like how we handled the structured-input parsing in that invoicing module we shipped last quarter, but for time logs.

The tricky parts are around the clock time handling — people write times in all sorts of ways (12h, 24h, crossing midnight, etc.) and we need to support all of that gracefully. There's also the question of what happens when someone leaves a timer running and never closes it — we need to handle that 'open' state sensibly depending on the current time. Date grouping (weeks, months, quarters, years) needs to work correctly even at year boundaries. We also want to flag likely mistakes — duplicate open timers, overlapping time blocks, future-dated entries, that kind of thing.

Outputs should include parsed structure, totals, diffs against targets, filtered views by date range or tag, and sorted listings. The whole thing should be well-organized code, not one giant file. We'll have a thin JSON adapter for testing but the core logic should be totally separate from that.

One more pass on the edge cases the team asked about. For duration formatting, zero minutes needs to come out as '0m' everywhere, including the signed version. So even in signed form we do not stick anything on it — it stays '0m', not '+0m'. On parsing, if a duration includes both hours and minutes, that minute piece has to stay in the 0–59 range. So '1h60m' is rejected with error 'unrepresentable_duration'. But if there’s no hour part, minutes can go past 59 just fine, so '120m' parses to 120 minutes without error.

On the open-range behavior in hypothetical_total, let’s keep it narrow. An open range only gets closed at the reference timestamp if the record date is the same day as the reference timestamp or the day before. If it’s from an earlier record, it does not count. The 'ongoing' flag is true only when an open range was actually closed and contributed time to the total. The reference value for this is the 'until' ISO-8601 timestamp parameter, and yes, the reference timestamp is provided as an ISO-8601 string with UTC offset. This is the same rule for the hypothetical_total feature (feature9_3): open ranges are closed at a reference timestamp only when the record date is the same day or the previous day relative to the reference; controlled by the 'until' ISO-8601 timestamp parameter.

For filtering, date ranges are inclusive, so records on the boundary dates stay in. Tag filtering is a little more specific than just “record matches or not”: a record is included if its summary OR any of its entries contains the required tags, but the total_minutes in a tag-filtered result should count only the minutes from entries that match the tag, not the full record total. If a record ends up with no tag-matching entries, exclude it entirely.

On week grouping, we’re using ISO weeks, so Monday through Sunday. Given any date, period_week returns the Monday and Sunday of the week containing that date as since/until, plus the Monday and Sunday of the immediately preceding week as previous_since/previous_until. This needs to behave correctly across month and year edges, so for example 2009-01-02 belongs to the week starting 2008-12-29.

Warnings are just four types, and each one reports its kind and the date of the offending record. Those are (1) 'unclosed_open_range' — a record in the past (before the reference date) that contains an open range entry; (2) 'future_entry' — any record dated after the reference timestamp's date; (3) 'overlapping_ranges' — two or more time ranges within a single record whose intervals overlap; (4) 'record_exceeds_day' — a record whose total tracked time exceeds 1440 minutes (24 hours). Also, keeping the exact phrasing here because someone asked, s date; (3) is part of that list exactly as written above.

For the plain-text output, canonical serialisation should use 4-space indentation for entries beneath a record date, and continuation summary lines for an entry use 8-space indentation. Range formatting has a single space on each side of the dash, exactly like '11:25 - 17:10'. And the should-total annotation format is '(Xh Ym!)' using the compact duration form inside the parentheses and then the !.

On parse errors, each one should report 'code', 'line', 'position', and 'length'. 'line' is 1-based. 'position' is a 0-based character offset within the line excluding leading indentation/whitespace. 'length' is the number of erroneous characters. The example to follow is: in '2020-01-01 (asdf!)' the malformed should-total 'asdf' starts at position 12 (after '2020-01-01 (') and has length 4.

And just to restate the architecture bit since it came up again, the JSON stdin/stdout adapter pattern described in the Architecture section still stands: a thin execution adapter translates JSON commands into domain calls without coupling core logic to I/O — see the 'Strict Separation of Concerns' NFR in start.md.

trigger_keywords: what does 24:00 mean as a time
midnight next day parsing
special case for end of day time
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The special time '24:00' is valid and means midnight of the next day: hour=0, minute=0, day_shift=+1. '<24:00' means midnight of the current day: hour=0, minute=0, day_shift=0. Any other value like '25:12' or '3:60' that is syntactically valid but out of range yields 'invalid_time'.

trigger_keywords: day shift markers on time
previous day time prefix
next day time suffix meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A time string may carry a day-shift marker: '<' as a prefix means the time belongs to the previous day (day_shift=-1), '>' as a suffix means the time belongs to the next day (day_shift=+1). Shift markers on both sides simultaneously is malformed and yields 'malformed_time'. The midnight-offset of a shifted time projects it onto a continuous axis: day_shift=-1 makes the offset negative, day_shift=+1 makes it exceed 1440.

trigger_keywords: add duration to time crossing day boundary
what is impossible_operation for time addition
shifting time more than one day
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Adding minutes to a time is only valid if the result stays within one day-shift of the original (i.e. the result's day_shift relative to the base date is at most 1 in either direction, bounded by -1 to +1). If adding minutes would push the result more than one day away, the operation is rejected as 'impossible_operation'. For example, adding 1 minute to '23:59>' (already day_shift=+1) would require day_shift=+2, which is rejected.

trigger_keywords: ISO week year boundary
week number near end of year
week_year differs from calendar year
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The ISO week number and its associated week_year may differ from the calendar year near year boundaries. For example, 2021-01-01 falls in ISO week 53 of year 2020, so week_year=2020 and week_number=53 even though the calendar year is 2021. The weekday uses Monday=1 through Sunday=7.

trigger_keywords: tag extraction rules
what characters are valid in a hashtag
tag termination character
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A tag starts with '#' followed by one or more letters, digits, or underscores. It ends at the first character outside that set. Tags are returned lowercased, de-duplicated, and sorted alphabetically. For example, '#GREAT-ish' yields only '#great' because the hyphen terminates the tag. A '#' not followed by at least one valid character does not form a tag.

trigger_keywords: fuzzy tag matching with ellipsis
prefix match on tag query
tag query ending in three dots
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A tag query ending in '...' is a fuzzy prefix match: it succeeds if any extracted tag starts with the given prefix (case-insensitively, with or without a leading '#'). A plain query (no '...') is an exact match. The query is echoed back in the output exactly as supplied, and matches is true/false. For example, 'WoRl...' matches '#world' because 'world' starts with 'worl'.

trigger_keywords: record summary vs entry summary validation rules
difference between record-level and entry-level summary
blank first line in summary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Record-level summaries (text beneath the date line) require every line to be non-empty and must not begin with any whitespace character — a leading space triggers 'malformed_summary'. Entry-level summaries (text after/beside an entry) allow the first line to be empty or blank, but every subsequent line must be non-empty and non-blank. A trailing empty line after content is thus invalid for entry summaries.

trigger_keywords: open range in parsed document
entry type for unclosed timer
open_range minutes value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An open range (e.g. '11:00 - ?') is a distinct entry type called 'open_range'. Its minutes contribution is always 0 in a normal parse/total — it only contributes time in the hypothetical_total operation, where it is closed at a provided reference timestamp. Only one open range per record is allowed; a second one triggers a 'duplicate_open_range' parse error.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'filter', 'sort', 'period_week', 'period_month', 'period_quarter', 'period_year', 'warnings', 'format_duration', 'parse_duration', 'parse_time', 'add_duration_to_time', 'time_midnight_offset', 'parse_date', 'date_attributes', 'add_days', 'range_duration', 'extract_tags', 'match_tag', 'validate_record_summary', 'validate_entry_summary', 'parse_document', 'parse_errors', 'serialise_roundtrip', 'total', 'should_total_diff', 'hypothetical_total'. Each value maps to one feature.

record[1].entry[0].type=duration

context_pointers.vague_reference: date grouping needs to work correctly even at year boundaries
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ISO week boundary edge case in feature3_2 and feature11_1: week_year can differ from calendar year (e.g. 2021-01-01 is week 53 of 2020), and period_week must correctly straddle month/year boundaries

context_pointers.vague_reference: flag likely mistakes — duplicate open timers, overlapping time blocks, future-dated entries
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The warnings feature (feature12): four warning kinds — unclosed_open_range, overlapping_ranges, future_entry, record_exceeds_day — checked against a 'now' reference ISO-8601 timestamp

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the spec to confirm the exact behavior. Try searching for related test cases or the relevant feature section in the PRD.

feature10_1_filter.json.description: Filter records in a document. A record is kept only if it satisfies all of the supplied clauses: on/after a date, on/before a date, and containing all of a set of tags (tags may match the record summary or be narrowed to matching entries). The result reports the count, the matching dates, and the total minutes of the matching (possibly tag-reduced) records.

feature10_2_sort.json.description: Sort the records of a document by date, either oldest-first (ascending) or newest-first (descending), returning the resulting date order.

feature11_1_period_week.json.description: Given a date, compute the Monday-to-Sunday calendar week that contains it, plus the preceding week, expressed as inclusive start/end dates. Weeks may straddle month and year boundaries.

feature11_2_period_month.json.description: Given a calendar month written as YYYY-MM, compute its inclusive first/last day and those of the preceding month, accounting for varying month lengths and leap years; reject malformed month strings.

feature11_3_period_quarter.json.description: Given a date, compute the calendar quarter (three months) that contains it, plus the preceding quarter, expressed as inclusive start/end dates.

feature11_4_period_year.json.description: Given a calendar year written as YYYY, compute its inclusive first/last day and those of the preceding year; reject malformed or out-of-range year strings.

feature12_warnings.json.description: Scan records against a reference timestamp for likely mistakes and report each warning's neutral kind and the date of the offending record. Checks cover unclosed open ranges in the past, entries dated in the future, overlapping time ranges within a record, and records whose total exceeds 24 hours.

feature1_1_format_duration.json.description: Render a signed minute amount into the canonical compact duration string, both without and with an explicit leading plus sign.

feature1_2_parse_duration.json.description: Parse a duration string into a signed total number of minutes; reject malformed text and minute parts that exceed 59 when an hour part is present.

feature2_1_parse_time.json.description: Parse a wall-clock time string into its hour, minute, day-shift (yesterday/today/tomorrow) and clock convention (24-hour vs 12-hour), including the 24:00 boundary; reject malformed and out-of-range values.

feature2_2_add_duration_to_time.json.description: Add a signed minute amount to a wall-clock time, allowing the result to shift to the adjacent day; reject shifts of more than one day in either direction.

feature2_3_time_midnight_offset.json.description: Compute the number of minutes from midnight to a wall-clock time; times shifted to the previous day are negative, times shifted to the next day exceed a full day.

feature3_1_parse_date.json.description: Parse a calendar date string in either dashed (YYYY-MM-DD) or slashed (YYYY/MM/DD) form, preserving which separator style was used; reject malformed strings and calendar-invalid dates.

feature3_2_date_attributes.json.description: Given a calendar date by its numeric year/month/day, derive its weekday (Monday=1..Sunday=7), its quarter (1-4), and its ISO week number together with the year that week number belongs to; reject calendar-invalid dates.

feature3_3_add_days.json.description: Add (or subtract) a number of days to a calendar date, rolling correctly across month and year boundaries and accounting for leap years.

feature4_range_duration.json.description: Build a time range from a start and end wall-clock time and compute its serialised form and its duration in minutes; ranges may span the adjacent day via shifted times; reject ranges whose end precedes their start.

feature5_1_extract_tags.json.description: Extract all hashtags from one or more lines of free text, returning them lowercased, de-duplicated and sorted; a tag is a '#' followed by letters, digits or underscores and ends at the first other character.

feature5_2_match_tag.json.description: Test whether a set of extracted tags contains a query tag. An exact query matches a whole tag; a query ending in '...' matches any tag that starts with the given prefix (fuzzy match).

feature6_1_validate_record_summary.json.description: Validate a record-level summary (the text under the date). Every line must be non-empty and must not begin with any blank character (space, tab, or other Unicode space separators).

feature6_2_validate_entry_summary.json.description: Validate an entry-level summary (the text behind an entry). The first line may be empty or blank, but every subsequent line must be non-empty and non-blank.

feature7_1_parse_document.json.description: Parse a plain-text document into structured records. Each record exposes its date, optional should-total (in minutes), optional record summary, and its ordered entries; each entry exposes its kind (duration / range / open_range), serialised value, duration in minutes, and summary (lines joined by '|').

cases.expected_output:
records=1
record[0].date=1970-08-29
record[0].should_total=495
record[0].summary=Record summary
record[0].entries=3
record[0].entry[0].type=duration
record[0].entry[0].value=1h
record[0].entry[0].minutes=60
record[0].entry[0].summary=Work
record[0].entry[1].type=range
record[0].entry[1].value=8:00 - 9:30
record[0].entry[1].minutes=90
record[0].entry[1].summary=
record[0].entry[2].type=open_range
record[0].entry[2].value=11:00 - ?
record[0].entry[2].minutes=0
record[0].entry[2].summary=|Open range

records=2
record[0].date=1999-05-31
record[0].should_total=0
record[0].summary=
record[0].entries=0
record[1].date=1999-06-03
record[1].should_total=0
record[1].summary=
record[1].entries=1
record[1].entry[0].type=duration
record[1].entry[0].value=1h
record[1].entry[0].minutes=60
record[1].entry[0].summary=

feature7_2_parse_errors.json.description: Report the structural errors found while parsing a document. Each error carries a neutral category, the 1-based line number, the cursor position within the line (excluding indentation), and the number of erroneous characters.

feature8_serialise_roundtrip.json.description: Parse a document and re-serialise it into the canonical text representation. The canonical form uses 4-space indentation, a single space around range dashes, and places continuation summary lines at double indentation.

input.text:
1999-05-31 (8h30m!)
Summary that consists of multiple
lines and contains a #tag as well.
    5h30m This and that
    -2h Something else
    <18:00 - 4:00 Foo
        Bar
    19:00 - 20:00
    20:01 - 0:15>
    1:00am - 3:12pm
    7:00 - ?

cases.expected_output:
1999-05-31 (8h30m!)
Summary that consists of multiple
lines and contains a #tag as well.
    5h30m This and that
    -2h Something else
    <18:00 - 4:00 Foo
        Bar
    19:00 - 20:00
    20:01 - 0:15>
    1:00am - 3:12pm
    7:00 - ?

feature9_1_total.json.description: Compute the total tracked time (in minutes) across all records in a document by summing every entry; durations and ranges contribute, open ranges contribute zero.

feature9_2_should_total_diff.json.description: Compute the aggregate should-total of all records, the actual total, and the difference (actual minus should), all in minutes.

feature9_3_hypothetical_total.json.description: Compute the total tracked time as if every open range were closed at a given reference timestamp, and report whether an open range was actually counted (i.e. the record is ongoing). Open ranges only count if their record is the reference day or the day before.
